old_claim.note: Scholasticism provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Renaissance humanism formed against the medieval Scholastic program: Petrarch emphasized classical authors over Scholastic thinkers and rejected scholastic dialectical subtlety, so the edge records inherited intellectual context rather than a direct enabling capability.

source_shape.source_locator: Top Questions and Classical studies sections: Britannica says Petrarch emphasized classical authors over Scholastic thinkers, then rejected scholastic dialectical subtleties and turned to the moral weight of the Classical world.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Petrarch's humanism is framed as a turn from medieval Scholasticism toward classical authors and classical moral examples.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source supports Scholasticism as the inherited medieval intellectual context humanism reacted against, not as a direct technical enabler.

ontology_before: The old edge treated Scholasticism as a generic enabling capability for humanism.
ontology_after: The corrected edge models Scholasticism as the medieval intellectual predecessor and foil against which Petrarchan humanism defined itself.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed edge type: scholasticism is now historical_predecessor rather than enabling.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to scholastic humanism rather than Petrarchan and Renaissance humanism.
A stronger source shows Scholasticism directly supplied the core method of Renaissance humanism.
The graph later adds a separate reaction-against edge type, making historical_predecessor too broad.
short_reason: Scholasticism is the inherited intellectual context and foil, not a generic enabling capability.
